roots.title: Course Logistics, Sparse Recovery in Black Hole Imaging, and Holiday Announcement
roots.summary: An introduction to Data 100 at UC Berkeley covered course logistics, including fire code regulations for seating and options for Zoom participation. Instructions were provided for using the Slido platform for attendance and questions, with TAs available for support. The lecture discussed sparse recovery techniques in black hole imaging, emphasizing the need for prior knowledge due to incomplete data, and illustrated the consequences of incorrect assumptions on algorithm outputs. The segment referenced the first image of a black hole captured in 2019, highlighting technological advancements. The instructor concluded with a holiday weekend wish and a reminder for students to return on Tuesday.

children.title: Course Logistics and Attendance Procedures
children.summary: An introduction to Data 100 at UC Berkeley emphasized the need for students to find seats or exit the lecture hall to adhere to fire code regulations. Options for synchronous participation via Zoom were provided. Instructions for scanning a QR code to access the Slido platform for attendance, polls, and question submissions were given, along with the availability of TAs for assistance.

children.title: Course Logistics and Attendance Procedures
children.summary: An introduction to Data 100 at UC Berkeley was provided, highlighting the importance of finding seats or leaving the lecture hall to comply with fire code regulations. Students were informed about the option to join via Zoom for synchronous participation. Instructions were given for scanning a QR code to access the Slido platform for attendance, polls, and question submissions, with TAs available for assistance.

children.title: Course Logistics and Attendance Procedures
children.summary: An introduction to Data 100 at UC Berkeley, emphasizing the need for students to find seats or leave the lecture hall due to fire code regulations. Students can join via Zoom for synchronous participation. Instructions were provided for scanning a QR code to access the Slido platform for attendance and polls, as well as for submitting questions, with TAs available for assistance.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Good morning, data 100 welcome to the largest upper division course at UC Berkeley. This is very exciting. Before we get started, I would like to ask everyone to either find a seat or please leave the lecture hall because of the fire code. And you should be able to join over Zoom and then watch us synchronously with us. And maybe next time we'll try to find a way to accommodate most people. There are some seats over here, there's some in the back, and I see like a cluster of small number of seats here. So either find a seat or please leave the lecture, and I'm sorry about that. This is kind of one part of the perk of having a large force, and this is the largest kind of lecture hall that we could get. All right. So you saw few QR codes that was posted online. Please go ahead. Scan this QR code. This is a Slido platform that we're using for kind of doing lecture attendance. If you're on your phone, scan it. If you're on your laptop, just go to Slido.com and then type in those digits. And you should be able to get in and then see all the polls that were posting. This is also where you can ask questions. And our wonderful TA's are here for responding to questions that you might have in kind of in a synchronous way. Video: 1: Lec 01 - DS100 Fa24 - Course C >

children.title: Sparse Recovery Techniques in Black Hole Imaging
children.summary: The application of sparse recovery algorithms for processing signals from black holes is discussed, highlighting the necessity of prior knowledge due to incomplete information. A hypothetical scenario illustrates the impact of incorrect assumptions about black holes on algorithm outputs. The segment also references the first image of a black hole captured in 2019, showcasing advancements in technology and methodology in this field.

children.title: Sparse Recovery Techniques in Black Hole Imaging
children.summary: The application of sparse recovery algorithms for processing signals from black holes is discussed, emphasizing the importance of prior knowledge due to incomplete information. A hypothetical scenario illustrates how incorrect assumptions about black holes can affect algorithm outputs. The segment also references the first image of a black hole captured in 2019, highlighting advancements in technology and methodology in this area.

children.title: Sparse Recovery Techniques for Black Hole Imaging
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the application of sparse recovery algorithms to process signals from black holes, leading to the creation of images of these astronomical phenomena. It highlights the necessity of utilizing prior knowledge due to incomplete information and presents a hypothetical scenario where an incorrect assumption about black holes could influence the algorithms' outputs. The segment also references the first image of a black hole captured in 2019, emphasizing the technological and methodological advancements in this field.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Sparse data. signals from a black hole or signals around around a black hole. And then using ideas that we'll actually cover in this class. Deloping algorithms to take those signals and turn them into the first picture of a black hole. So the sparse recovery items that we will cover here are the same kinds of techniques being used to solve these problems. That's kind of neat about this is we have to use our parent knowledge because we don't have all the information. So this day was saying, you know, I assume black holes were actually snowmen. What would happen if I ran that actual image through these algorithms? And some algorithms, you know, have this paradox, it should be a hole, and they inject a hole in the picture even though there isn't one. Video: 1: Lec 01 - DS100 Fa24 - Course 0 X
